items.q: Does passing all its tests prove a program has no bugs?
items.correct: No - testing can show the presence of errors but never their complete absence; untested paths and unanticipated inputs can still hide bugs.
items.misconception: Yes - a full pass means the program is certified bug-free.
items.random_wrong: Yes - one successful run with normal data proves correctness.
items.why: Tests sample behaviour rather than prove it. Good testers maximise coverage with normal, boundary and erroneous data, but certainty is unobtainable.

items.q: Does validation guarantee the data entered is true?
items.correct: No - validation only confirms data is reasonable and within the rules; a user can type a wrong but valid value, which is why verification exists.
items.misconception: Yes - validated data is always accurate.
items.random_wrong: Yes - validation checks data against official records.
items.why: Plausible and correct get conflated. A date of birth of 01/01/2000 passes every format and range check while still being the wrong person's birthday.

items.q: If users follow the instructions, is defensive design unnecessary?
items.correct: No - defensive design exists precisely because real users make mistakes and attackers deliberately misuse programs; robust code assumes bad input will arrive.
items.misconception: Yes - well-written instructions remove the need for validation.
items.random_wrong: Yes - defensive design only matters for games.
items.why: Designing for the ideal user is the classic trap. Robust programs treat all input as untrusted - the core principle behind anticipating misuse.

items.q: A range check is an example of:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Input validation. “Authentication” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.correct: Values at the very edge of the acceptable range
items.why: Correct answer: Values at the very edge of the acceptable range. “Values chosen completely at random” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: A program that runs without crashing but produces the wrong answer contains:
items.why: Correct answer: A logic error. “A syntax error” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Which of these improves the maintainability of a program?
items.correct: Comments, sensible variable names and indentation
items.misconception: Removing all spaces from the code
items.why: Correct answer: Comments, sensible variable names and indentation. “Removing all spaces from the code” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: A program accepts whole-number ages from 1 to 100 inclusive. What is the largest valid boundary value to test?
items.why: The real figure is 100. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
